name: Golang Concurrency Interview Prep
description: 20 high-level concurrency questions frequently asked in Big Tech interviews.

cards.front: 1. What is the difference between concurrency and parallelism?
cards.back: Concurrency is dealing with lots of things at once (composition of tasks); Parallelism is doing lots of things at once (execution of tasks).

cards.front: 2. How does the Go scheduler (G-M-P model) work?
cards.back: G (Goroutine), M (Machine/Thread), and P (Processor/Context) work together to multiplex many goroutines onto fewer OS threads.

cards.front: 3. What happens if you send to a nil channel?
cards.back: Sending to a nil channel blocks the goroutine forever, causing a deadlock if no other path exists.

cards.front: 4. What is the purpose of the `select` statement?
cards.back: `select` lets a goroutine wait on multiple communication operations, choosing the first one that is ready.

cards.front: 5. Can you have a race condition in a thread-safe language like Go?
cards.back: Yes. Using shared memory without proper synchronization (mutexes, channels) leads to race conditions even with Go's memory model.

cards.front: 6. What is the difference between `sync.Mutex` and `sync.RWMutex`?
cards.back: `RWMutex` allows multiple readers or a single writer, whereas `Mutex` allows only one goroutine access regardless of the operation.

cards.front: 7. What is a "buffered channel" and when should you use it?
cards.back: A buffered channel holds a specific number of elements; it is used to decouple sender/receiver speed and prevent blocking.

cards.front: 8. How do you detect race conditions in Go?
cards.back: Use the built-in race detector by running your code with the `-race` flag: `go run -race main.go`.

cards.front: 9. How do you gracefully stop a goroutine?
cards.back: Pass a `context.Context` to the goroutine and listen to its `Done()` channel to trigger a shutdown.

cards.front: 10. Why is `sync.WaitGroup` used?
cards.back: It is used to wait for a collection of goroutines to finish their execution before proceeding.

cards.front: 11. What is the danger of `time.Sleep` in production code?
cards.back: It is imprecise and inefficient; using `context` or channels for synchronization is preferred for responsiveness.

cards.front: 12. How does Go’s memory model handle synchronization?
cards.back: It defines the "happens-before" relationship to guarantee memory visibility between goroutines.

cards.front: 13. What is the `fan-out` and `fan-in` pattern?
cards.back: Fan-out distributes work across multiple goroutines; fan-in multiplexes their results back into a single channel.

cards.front: 14. What are the common pitfalls when using channels?
cards.back: Deadlocks, leaking goroutines, sending to/closing nil/closed channels.

cards.front: 15. How do you implement a singleton pattern in Go?
cards.back: Use `sync.Once` to ensure a piece of code (like initialization) runs exactly once.

cards.front: 16. What is an atomic operation and why use `sync/atomic`?
cards.back: Atomic operations are CPU-level instructions that complete without interruption; they are faster than mutexes for simple counter increments.

cards.front: 17. What is a "goroutine leak"?
cards.back: A goroutine that starts but never finishes or sends data back, remaining in memory indefinitely.

cards.front: 18. How does `sync.Map` differ from a standard `map` with a `Mutex`?
cards.back: `sync.Map` is optimized for concurrent scenarios where keys are often stable (read-heavy or write-once-read-many).

cards.front: 19. What is the purpose of `runtime.Gosched()`?
cards.back: It yields the processor, allowing other goroutines to run.

cards.front: 20. When should you prefer channels over mutexes?
cards.back: Use channels when passing ownership of data or coordinating sequences; use mutexes for protecting internal state/caching.

author: Gemini
